narrative:
La fragilidad y la incertidumbre en torno a la durabilidad del alto al fuego entre Estados Unidos e Irán constituyen el driver principal que domina el escenario actual. Este alto al fuego, vigente de manera precaria por dos semanas, es relevante porque condiciona la estabilidad geopolítica en una región clave para el suministro energético global. A su vez, las tensiones persistentes en el Estrecho de Hormuz, donde el tráfico marítimo permanece muy deprimido y con la amenaza de un peaje iraní, junto con el conflicto en Líbano y la campaña continua contra Hezbollah, generan presiones inflacionarias y elevan las primas de riesgo que afectan la percepción global de riesgo y la dinámica económica.

El mercado ha reaccionado con un rebote técnico en los principales índices accionarios, como el S&P 500, aprovechando el respiro temporal que ofrece la continuidad del alto al fuego. Sin embargo, esta recuperación se da en un contexto de alta volatilidad y riesgos latentes, dado que las tensiones en el Estrecho de Hormuz y en Líbano mantienen un elevado nivel de incertidumbre. La inflación en Estados Unidos se ha acelerado, reflejada en un aumento del 3.3% interanual del CPI en marzo, lo que ha generado preocupación sobre la persistencia de presiones inflacionarias globales y la posible respuesta más restrictiva de la política monetaria.

Desde una lectura institucional, el escenario apunta a un entorno macroeconómico complejo, caracterizado por un riesgo creciente de estagflación, donde la inflación se mantiene al alza mientras el crecimiento económico se debilita. El capital institucional se posiciona con cautela, favoreciendo la exposición a activos que puedan beneficiarse de la recuperación técnica en acciones, pero con una gestión activa del riesgo ante la posibilidad de nuevas escaladas geopolíticas. La continuidad del alto al fuego, aunque frágil, limita la aversión extrema al riesgo, pero la imposición del peaje en Hormuz y la persistencia del conflicto en Líbano sostienen la presión sobre los precios del petróleo y la inflación, condicionando la política monetaria estadounidense hacia un endurecimiento que reduce el espacio para recortes de tasas en el corto plazo.

Los eventos clave que pueden validar o modificar esta narrativa incluyen la reunión programada para el 11 de abril entre funcionarios de Estados Unidos e Irán en Pakistán, que podría consolidar o romper el alto al fuego; la publicación del CPI de marzo el 10 de abril, que confirmará la tendencia inflacionaria; y la evolución del tránsito marítimo en el Estrecho de Hormuz, donde cualquier medida iraní para imponer peajes o incentivar el tráfico tendrá un impacto directo en los precios energéticos. Además, el desarrollo de las negociaciones entre Israel y Líbano será un factor determinante para la estabilidad regional. La ruptura del alto al fuego, un fracaso en las negociaciones o una escalada en cualquiera de estos frentes podrían revertir rápidamente la percepción de riesgo y la dinámica de mercado.
regime:
🏷️ MARKET REGIME: Régimen Transicional / Volátil 🟡

El mercado muestra rebotes técnicos comprados, pero con alta sensibilidad a la información y movimientos erráticos. Las rupturas de niveles clave tienden a fallar o revertirse rápidamente, reflejando incertidumbre y falta de continuidad en las tendencias. La volatilidad es elevada y las reacciones a noticias generan oscilaciones amplias, sin un sesgo claro y sostenido. En situaciones como esta, típicamente los traders deben esperar movimientos impredecibles y gestionar riesgos con disciplina, priorizando la flexibilidad sobre posiciones direccionales fuertes.

vital.source_subject: Vital Knowledge: Vital Dawn for Friday April 10, 2026
vital.market_levels:
Market levels – the US equity futures are seeing losses so far this morning: S&P -8.75 points/13bp, Dow -96 points/20bp, Nasdaq -33 points/13bp, and R2 K -7.3 points/28bp. The major Eurozone indices area flat-to-up 10bp, with outperformance in luxury, tech, media, and healthcare while utilities, energy, telecoms, insurance, and basic resources underperform . Stocks in Asia generally saw gains on Friday: TPX -0.04%, NKY +1.84%, Hang Seng +0.55%, HSCEI +0.5%, SHCOMP +0.51%, Shenzhen +1.54%, Taiwan TAIEX +1.6%, Korea KOSPI +1.4%, Australia ASX 200 -0.14%, and India is up ~90bp. Treasuries are for sale, w/yields up ~2-3bp across the curve ahead of the Mar CPI that’s expected to be very hot . The market is pricing in ~6bp of Fed rate cuts this year. The DXY is ticking up 13bp following several days of declines. Brent is rising ~220bp to ~$98 while gold falls 50bp and silver drops 30bp. Bitcoin is sliding 100bp to ~$71.66 K.
•
vital.whats_happening:
What’s happening this morning – it’s fairly quiet as investors remain on the edge of their seats regarding the Middle East ceasefire ahead of the upcoming talks between US and Iranian officials in Pakistan (Iranian officials are on the ground in Pakistan according to reports, and the meeting is set to occur early Sat morning Pakistan time). The two big impediments to a deal include Hormuz (traffic remains very depressed, and Tehran seems to be pushing forward with plans for a toll, something Trump Thurs night warned against) and Lebanon (Netanyahu agreed to dial back the intensity of IDF strikes and will hold talks with the Lebanon gov’t, but Israel vowed to continue its campaign against Hezbollah). In terms of non-Iran news , we didn’t see anything too dramatic since the Thurs close ( China’s inflation was mixed, the Bank of Korea left rates unchanged as expected, TSMC’s Q1 revenue came in a bit better w/Mar sales surging ~45% Y/Y vs. ~22% in Feb, The Information published Space X P&L details from 2025 that seem to fall far short of the types of numbers required for a ~$1.75 T+ market cap, and the latest Barron’s cover story talks about a Space X/Tesla merger ).
•
vital.thinking_about_markets:
How we’re thinking about markets – there’s still a lot of cynicism and skepticism about the durability of the ceasefire and to the extent the détente’s permanency outperforms expectations (which is our view), this will place further upward pressure on the SPX , especially given the technical backdrop (characterized by lean long positioning and negative sentiment). However, the SPX has already bounced ~8% trough-to-peak in just a few days, and the index is nearing the ~6900-7000 zone , which was a problem area even before the war started . For US earnings , the key reports include: Mon morning ( GS ); Tues morning ( BLK, C, JNJ, JPM, KMX , and WFC ); Wed morning ( BAC, PNC , and MS ); Wed night ( SLG ); Thurs morning ( ABT, BK, CFG, KEY, MRSH, PEP, SCHW, TRV , and USB ); Thurs night ( AA and NFLX ); and Fri morning ( RF, STT , and TFC ). In Europe, the key earnings reports include: Mon night ( LVMH ), Tues night ( Kering ); Wed morning ( ASML and Hermes ); Thurs morning ( Entain , Pernod Ricard, and TSMC ); and Fri morning ( Ericsson ).

Iran

• What bulls are talking about : while there were ceasefire violations earlier Wed, on Thurs and into Fri the détente between the US/Israel and Iran was holding in place reasonably well . Trump told NBC News that he was “very optimistic” about reaching a deal w/Iran (“Iran’s leaders talk much differently when you’re at a meeting than they do to the press. They’re much more reasonable. They’re agreeing to all the things that they have to agree to”). Importantly, it looks like the situation in Lebanon could cool , removing a substantial source of pressure from the Iran ceasefire . Netanyahu on Thurs said Israel was seeking to “ open direct negotiations with Lebanon as soon as possible ” (according to an NBC News report, Trump asked Netanyahu to dial back the intensity of the IDF’s strikes on Lebanon, and the Israeli PM agreed “to be a helpful partner” ).
• What bears are talking about : the Middle East ceasefire is very fragile and risks being undermined by the situation in Lebanon ( Netanyahu might be seeking to hold talks w/Lebanon, but he was explicit in stating that no cease fire exists between the two countries , and vowed to continue striking Hezbollah “with force” ) and Hormuz ( volumes through the waterway remain depressed , and Tehran seems very serious about proceeding with plans for a toll, something Trump at 5:10pm ET Thurs afternoon warned them not to do). The US and Iran have some huge differences that need to be ironed out for a full deal to be implemented, and time is very much of the essence (Trump warned Wed night that the Pentagon will resume its campaign against Iran if an agreement isn’t reached ). Even if the US and Iran are able to secure a permanent détente, the situation in the Middle East won’t go back to the way it was in the near or immediate term – every commodity and product going through Hormuz will embed a significant geopolitical risk premium for the foreseeable future , ensuring the global economy faces a persistent source of stagflationary pressure . Significant damage was inflicted to key infrastructure in the Middle East over the last several weeks, and it will take a long time to bring all this back online ( Saudi Arabia on Thurs said it lost ~600 K BPD of oil capacity due to damage from the war). The IMF on Thurs warned that the Middle East war will deal a major blow to the global economy , even with the recent ceasefire.

• Trump is demanding commitments from NATO allies to help provide support for securing Hormuz ( Reuters )

reuters.body_excerpt:
Plus, shippers weigh unusual routes.

Daily Briefing

Daily Briefing

By Kate Turton

Hello. The US-Iran ceasefire is under strain ahead of talks, and shippers weigh unusual routes as high air cargo rates and ocean gridlock persist.

Elsewhere, Putin announces an Orthodox Easter ceasefire , and this is how the AI boom derailed clean‑air efforts in one of America's most polluted cities.

Plus, a runaway wolf in South Korea

Today's Top News

Damaged buildings at Kafr Kila following Israeli army activity across the border between Israel and Lebanon. REUTERS/Ammar Awad

War in the Middle East

•

The Strait of Hormuz remained shut and Israel traded fire with Hezbollah in Lebanon, which the United States and Iran each described as violations of their ceasefire deal on the eve of their first peace talks of the war .

• US House of Representatives Republicans blocked a Democratic effort to end US attacks on Iran, as Donald Trump's party continued to prevent efforts to rein in the president's war powers .
•

Prime Minister Keir Starmer said the Iran war must become a turning point for Britain after two decades of crises, and he promised to strengthen the country's economy and military to cope with a more "volatile and dangerous" world.

In other news

•

Russian President Vladimir Putin announced a 32-hour ceasefire over a two-day period for Orthodox ‌Easter and his Ukrainian counterpart Volodymyr Zelenskiy said Kyiv would abide by the measure.
•

" Sometimes I could hear her voice just catch a little bit." White House reporter Bo Erickson was inside the room when Melania Trump delivered an emotional address distancing herself from Jeffrey Epstein. He tells the Reuters World News podcast about the surprising speech.

• The finale to NASA's celebrated 10-day mission is expected ‌to begin with the separation of Orion's crew capsule from its service module, followed by a fiery re-entry through Earth's atmosphere.
•

A court in Eswatini has ruled that the first five migrants the Trump administration sent to the African kingdom have a right to a lawyer , after they were denied legal representation on their transfer from the US to a Swazi jail in July.
•

Taiwanese officials are tracking what they view as a worrying rise in Chinese naval activity and military pressure against the island, even as Beijing presses a message of peace and cooperation in meetings with Taiwan's opposition leader.

Business & Markets

Cargo ship with shipping containers at the port of Oakland , Oakland, California. February 23, 2026. REUTERS/Carlos Barria/File Photo

•

Some shippers caught between high jet fuel prices and congestion in vital Middle East waterways are now looking for transport routes ‌in unexpected places , with a recent ceasefire between Iran and the United States unlikely to offer quick relief.

• A US trade court will consider the legality of a 10% global import tax imposed by the Trump administration, which several states and small businesses say sidesteps a US Supreme Court ruling ‌that invalidated most of Trump's previous tariffs.
•

Trump warned Tehran not to charge tolls on ships crossing the Strait of Hormuz, as a mounting global energy crisis prompted Japan to announce a further emergency oil release .
•

Meta said it is pulling ads from Facebook and Instagram aimed at recruiting new plaintiffs for ongoing litigation accusing it and other social media companies of designing their platforms ‌to be addictive to young users.
•

Diminishing demand for late-night drinking and rising operating costs mean London's nightclubs are having to work harder than ever to keep their doors open.

The Week Ahead

•
Voters in Hungary could deliver a boon to bond investors, while Peru's weekend polls will help determine the next step for its economy, the latest in a string of key elections worldwide this year .

• Finance chiefs from around the globe will gather in Washington for spring meetings of the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank Group.
•
Key Chinese data on Thursday will offer a first look at how the world's second biggest economy is adapting to a slower growth trajectory and the war.
•

Here's everything you need to know about the week ahead in financial markets.

How the AI boom derailed clean‑air efforts in one of America's most polluted cities

Barbara Johnson stands beside her husband as he uses an oxygen concentrator, Florissant, Missouri. REUTERS/Lawrence Bryant

Barbara Johnson has been fighting coal pollution for decades in her mostly Black neighborhood of North St. Louis as an organizer with Metropolitan Congregations United.

Until recently, she had reason to believe things would improve: tougher federal soot standards adopted in 2024 under the Biden administration were scheduled to go into effect in 2027, requiring plants to slash emissions or shut down. That would have forced one of the area’s biggest polluters - Ameren’s Labadie Energy Center power plant - to cut its soot emissions in half to stay in bus

filtered_signal.driver_principal: La fragilidad y la incertidumbre en torno a la durabilidad del alto al fuego entre Estados Unidos e Irán, junto con las tensiones persistentes en el Estrecho de Hormuz y el conflicto en Líbano, están generando presiones inflacionarias y riesgos geopolíticos que afectan la percepción de riesgo y el comportamiento de los mercados globales.
hechos_confirmados: Se mantiene un alto al fuego precario de dos semanas entre Estados Unidos e Irán, con negociaciones programadas en Pakistán para buscar un acuerdo más duradero.
El tráfico marítimo en el Estrecho de Hormuz sigue muy deprimido y cerrado, con planes de Irán para imponer un peaje a los barcos que atraviesen, lo que ha sido advertido por Estados Unidos.
Israel ha reducido la intensidad de sus ataques en Líbano y acordó iniciar negociaciones directas con el gobierno libanés, pero mantiene su campaña contra Hezbollah.
El índice de precios al consumidor (CPI) de Estados Unidos para marzo mostró una aceleración significativa, con un aumento del 3.3% interanual, reflejando presiones inflacionarias crecientes.
filtered_signal.escenario_dominante: Existe una alta probabilidad de que el alto al fuego entre Estados Unidos e Irán se mantenga de forma frágil en el corto plazo, permitiendo cierta recuperación en los mercados de acciones, aunque con volatilidad y riesgos latentes. Se espera que las tensiones en el Estrecho de Hormuz y en Líbano continúen generando primas de riesgo geopolítico que mantendrán presiones inflacionarias y afectarán la dinámica económica global.
filtered_signal.interpretacion_institucional: La persistencia del alto al fuego, aunque frágil, ofrece un respiro temporal para los mercados, favoreciendo un rebote técnico en índices como el S&P 500. Sin embargo, la imposición de peajes en Hormuz y la continuidad del conflicto en Líbano mantienen un riesgo geopolítico elevado que se traduce en un aumento sostenido de los precios del petróleo y presiones inflacionarias globales. Esto sugiere un entorno macroeconómico caracterizado por un riesgo de estagflación, con inflación al alza y crecimiento económico debilitado. La política monetaria estadounidense podría endurecerse ante la aceleración inflacionaria, limitando el espacio para recortes de tasas en el corto plazo. La negociación en Pakistán será clave para definir la evolución del conflicto y su impacto en los mercados.
riesgos_secundarios: El alto al fuego podría romperse debido a incidentes en Líbano o nuevas acciones militares en la región, escalando el conflicto.
Irán podría implementar efectivamente el peaje en Hormuz, afectando gravemente el comercio energético global y elevando aún más los precios del petróleo.
Las negociaciones entre Estados Unidos e Irán podrían fracasar, provocando una reanudación de hostilidades militares.
La presión política interna en Estados Unidos, incluyendo bloqueos legislativos y retrasos en confirmaciones clave, podría limitar la capacidad de respuesta y negociación del gobierno.
La participación o influencia de China en el conflicto o en las negociaciones podría complicar la dinámica geopolítica y comercial.
eventos_clave: Reunión entre funcionarios de Estados Unidos e Irán en Pakistán programada para el sábado 11 de abril de 2026.
Publicación del índice de precios al consumidor (CPI) de Estados Unidos para marzo el viernes 10 de abril de 2026 a las 8:30 am ET.
Audiencia en la Corte de Comercio Internacional de Estados Unidos sobre el arancel global del 10% impuesto por la administración Trump.
Decisión y evolución de la situación en el Estrecho de Hormuz respecto al tránsito marítimo y posibles medidas para incentivar el tráfico.
Desarrollo de las negociaciones entre Israel y Líbano para reducir la intensidad del conflicto y posibles acuerdos.
